deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 30-year-old man who's been started on haloperidol and is presenting with several symptoms.
Let me go through each clue step by step.
First, the patient was recently started on haloperidol, which is an antipsychotic medication, typically used for schizophrenia or other psychotic disorders.
But he's developed some concerning symptoms: hyperpyrexia (fever), muscle rigidity, akinesia (movement issues), mutism, excessive sweating, tachycardia (fast heart rate), and elevated blood pressure.
His lab results show an increased white blood cell count and elevated creatinine phosphokinase.
No other drugs are mentioned, and there's no sign of infection, so infection is unlikely.
I remember that antipsychotic medications can sometimes cause a condition called Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS).
NMS is a rare but severe reaction, usually occurring within the first few weeks of starting an antipsychotic.
Symptoms include hyperthermia (fever), muscle rigidity, altered consciousness, and sometimes autonomic issues like tachycardia and hypertension.
The elevated WBC and CK also fit with NMS, as leukocytosis and myoglobinuria (which can cause elevated CK) are common in this condition.
Since there's no history of infection and the timeline aligns with the onset after starting haloperidol, NMS seems likely.
Other possibilities like sepsis or heatstroke are less likely because there's no mention of infection or environmental factors.
Malignant hyperthermia is another consideration, but that's usually due to genetic factors or certain triggers like anesthesia, which isn't mentioned here.
So, putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis is Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome.

The timing of symptom onset after starting haloperidol points towards a medication-induced condition.
So, putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis is neuroleptic malignant syndrome.

Haloperidol is a typical antipsychotic, and one of the severe side effects is neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS).
The symptoms fit NMS: fever, rigidity, altered mental status (mutism, akinesia), autonomic instability, and lab abnormalities like leukocytosis and elevated CK.
The timing after starting the drug is also key.
Since there's no infection or other drugs, that makes NMS more likely.
Other possibilities could be serotonin syndrome, but that usually has more agitation, diarrhea, clonus, and different autonomic signs.
Malignant hyperthermia is triggered by anesthetics, which isn't mentioned here.
